EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a series of snow events in the lowlands and mountains of western Washington, a system off the Pacific brought a modifying airmass towards the area right before the Christmas holiday. Warmer Pacific air was able to overrun lingering cold air very near the surface, producing a warm layer of classic characteristic of a freezing rain profile. As such, measurable ice accumulation led to hazardous impacts across the area, most noticeably to roadways and travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant ice accumulation was reported form multiple sources during the morning hours of Dec 23. SeaTac airport reported around 0.20 inches of freezing rain by 8am on the 23rd. Other reports included: 0.25 inches in Des Moines, 0.13 inches at the NWS Seattle Office, 0.25 inches in Kenmore, 0.20 inches in Renton, and 0.13 inches in Mountlake Terrace. The highest amounts were reported from West Seattle at 0.3 inches, 0.3 inches in Federal Way, and 1 mile west of SeaTac at 0.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of wintry weather was noted across the Western Washington lowlands at the beginning of December, earlier than what is typically experienced. Snow began at the end of November with several rounds continuing through the first few days of September. While accumulations did not meet winter storm criteria Dec 1-2, widespread heavy snow accumulations were experienced on Dec 3. These conditions were largely due to Fraser outflow in the low levels. with deep upper troughing in the upper levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations across the Admiralty Inlet area reported light snow over the first several days of December. Observations Dec 1 and Dec 2 averaged around 1 inch or less, with an observation to the northwest of Port Ludlow reporting around 4 inches on December 3.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event brought heavy snow to the Cascades at the end of February, which resulted in the closure of the Passes in the Central Cascades as well as heightened avalanche danger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow to began to fall across the Central Cascades during the evening hours of February 26th. Snowfall remained fairly light before becoming much heavier throughout the day on the 27th. At Stevens Pass, 22 inches of new snow was reported between 6am on the 27th and 6am on the 28th. Snow would then continue through the 28th but lighten in intensity. Around 1am on the 28th, Stevens Pass closed due to snow and avalanche conditions.||At Snoqualmie Pass, snow was also heaviest on the 27th, with 19 inches of new snow reported during a 24 hour period from 6am on the 27th to 6am on the 28th. Snow would then taper through the 28th. Similar to Stevens Pass, shortly after midnight Snoqualmie Pass would close due to avalanche conditions both east bound and west bound at the summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event brought heavy snow to the Cascades at the end of February, which resulted in the closure of the Passes in the Central Cascades as well as heightened avalanche danger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began to fall across the Northern Cascades during the mid evening hours of February 27th. By 6am on the 28th, 19 inches of new snow was recorded at the Mount Baker Ski Resort and by 6pm, 25 inches of new snow had fallen. Snow would then continue through the final day in February and into the beginning of March, albeit much less heavy and impactful.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A natural avalanche was reported at Snoqualmie Pass along I-90 near the summit. The Pass had already been closed due to dangerous avalanche conditions.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the Cascades and Olympics , particularly the Central Cascades where Stevens Pass and Snoqualmie Pass was heavily impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling around Stevens Pass by mid-afternoon on February 19th and by 3am on the 20th, almost 20 inches of new snow was reported. Then by 3pm on the 20th, 24 inches of new snow was reported at the Pass. Snow then gradually tapered through the evening hours.||At Snoqualmie Pass, rain changed to snow during the late evening hours of the 19th as temperatures dropped below freezing. Although snowfall amounts did not reach winter storm criteria, about 10 inches of new snow fell within a 12 hour period after precipitation transition. Snow then eventually tapered during the afternoon hours of the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convergence zone lingering over Camano Island the morning of February 21 led to significant lowland snow accumulation. The convergence zone was forecast to affect mainly the Olympic Peninsula, but shifted east and heavily affected areas on the island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local CoCoRAHs observations showed an average of 3-5 inches of new snow accumulation across Camano Island after a strong convergence zone helped drag snow levels locally to the surface to produce a several hour period of heavy snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Irving Peak and White River Fires in the Cascades. Their proximity to each other allowed them to be combined into one incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White River and Irving Peak Fires began after a lightning storm passed through the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest on August 11 and was active through October. The Irving Peak and White River fires are located approximately 15 miles northwest of Plain, WA. They have burned around 11120 acres of timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Minnow Ridge Wildfire was started on September 9 by undetermined means.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnow Ridge Fire started on September 9 by undetermined factor. It burned through October. It is located 10 miles NE of Lake Wenatchee, WA. It burned 5350 acres rugged, steep timber terrain. Due to its proximity, it was grouped with White River and Irving Peak fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Parks Wildfire on August 21. It was added to the NW Pasayten Complex on September 6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parks Fire started on August 21 by lightning. It became part of the NW Pasayten Complex on September 6. It is located 30 miles NE of Mazama in rugged timber terrain. It burned 20759 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two strong Pacific fronts brought high winds to the coast as well as gusty winds to the Willamette Valley and interior lowlands of southwest Washington. This storm generated large seas impacting the coastline, and combined with rising river levels and high astronomical tides, resulted in some flooding along the coast due to poor drainage.  In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The seas at buoy 46029 (20NM West of Columbia River Mouth) rose above 26 feet at 11 seconds which would bring high surf conditions to the coastline. High Surf conditions continued at times through 1/3/2022 at 1640PST, then seas gradually subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two strong Pacific fronts brought high winds to the coast as well as gusty winds to the Willamette Valley and interior lowlands of southwest Washington. This storm generated large seas impacting the coastline, and combined with rising river levels and high astronomical tides, resulted in some flooding along the coast due to poor drainage.  In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very active frontal system brought heavy snow to the South Washington Cascades on the 2nd and 3rd with snow totals between 17 and 27 inches. Peak snow fall rates are estimated at 3 to 4 inches per hour. One particular site, June Lake SNOTEL (MRBW1) at 3440 feet had 34 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with ice accumulation resulted in the closure of SR-14, connecting western and eastern Oregon and Washington. Around 24 inches of snow fell in Stevenson, WA. In the Multnomah Falls area along I-84, 5 inches of snow fell with an unknown amount of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of strong Pacific fronts brought another round of heavy rain and resulting flooding of mainstem rivers draining the coastal mountains and interior of southwest Washington. Coastal flooding also occurred at some coastal communities as a result of high astronomical tides combined with high river levels and high seas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge on the Willapa River near Willapa rose above flood stage of 21.0 feet at 1620PST, crested at 23.3 feet on the 7th at 0345PST (major flood stage is 24.0 feet), then dropped below flood stage on the 7th at 1750PST.  As a result, 33 homes have significant damage (combined river and coastal flooding.) Two people were sheltered. Also landslides preventing road entries into the county including five slides on SR 105.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of strong Pacific fronts brought another round of heavy rain and resulting flooding of mainstem rivers draining the coastal mountains and interior of southwest Washington. Coastal flooding also occurred at some coastal communities as a result of high astronomical tides combined with high river levels and high seas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge on the Naselle River near Naselle rose above flood stage of 15.0 feet on the 6th, crested at 18.3 feet at 2000PST. This is above major flood stage of 17.0 feet. The river then dropped below flood stage on the 7th at 1130PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of strong Pacific fronts brought another round of heavy rain and resulting flooding of mainstem rivers draining the coastal mountains and interior of southwest Washington. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge on the Cowlitz River at Kelso rose above flood stage of 21.5 feet at 1330PST, crested at 24.0 feet at 2130PST, the dropped below flood stage on the 7th at 2300PST.  There were reports of several side road closed due to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over western Washington brought heavy rains to the coastal mountains in southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge on the Grays River near Rosburg rose above flood stage of 12.0 feet at 2345PST, crested at 15.5 feet on the 12th at 0715PST, the fell below flood stage at 2330PST.   A mudslide closed SR-4 in both direction near Raistakka Road.

In addition to river flooding, widespread areal flooding resulted in a lot of road closures in the county, trapping people for 3 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event, starting late Sunday February 27th and continuing through Monday February 28th, brought heavy rain and strong winds to northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Rainfall amounts varied from around one inch in the Willamette Valley, to 5 to 10 inches in the Coast Range as well as the Washington and northern Oregon Cascades. Widespread urban and small stream flooding occurred in the Greater Portland-Metro area, and rivers flooded along the coast, in particular the Grays, Wilson and Nehalem rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Grays River. Heavy rain fell as surrounding areas measured 2 to 10 inches of rain. No damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event, starting late Sunday February 27th and continuing through Monday February 28th, brought heavy rain and strong winds to northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Rainfall amounts varied from around one inch in the Willamette Valley, to 5 to 10 inches in the Coast Range as well as the Washington and northern Oregon Cascades. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Megler Bridge measured a max wind of 48 mph and gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a two day heavy snow event that impacted the Eastern Cascades in late February. Light snow arrived near the Cascades late February 26th. It spread across northern Washington into north Idaho on the 27th. A heavier round of precipitation arrived that evening into the 28th. It created hazardous travel conditions due to slick, snow covered roads. The two day event increased avalanche danger due to heavy wet snow falling on top of a layer of lighter snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 2 was closed at Stevens Pass for avalanche control at 0115 PST on February 28th due to heavy, wet snow. The pass remained closed until 1045 PST. Snow began mid-day in the Cascade Crest on February 27th. Stevens Pass recorded 22 inches of snow accumulation in the 24 hour period from 0700 PST February 27th to 0700 PST February 28th. In the same time period, the Lucerne and Stehekin COOP stations recorded between 20 and 24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a two day heavy snow event that impacted the Eastern Cascades in late February. Light snow arrived near the Cascades late February 26th. It spread across northern Washington into north Idaho on the 27th. A heavier round of precipitation arrived that evening into the 28th. It created hazardous travel conditions due to slick, snow covered roads. The two day event increased avalanche danger due to heavy wet snow falling on top of a layer of lighter snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began mid-day in the eastern Cascade valleys on February 27th. Numerous observation sites in Leavenworth and Plain recorded 14 to 16 inches of snow accumulation in the 24 hour time period from 0800 PST February 27th to 0800 PST February 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed over the weekend of February 19th and 20th. Moderate to heavy snow fell in the Cascades Passes, especially Stevens Pass. A majority of the snow fell overnight into the early morning hours of the 20th. Accumulations started in the evening due to road temperature cooling until after sunset. This led to winter driving conditions and the potential for avalanches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 2 was closed at Stevens Pass for avalanche control at 0430PST. Stevens Pass recorded 23 inches of snow accumulation in the morning of January 20th. A majority of the snowfall accumulated overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough with abundant moisture brought periodic heavy snow showers across the region, with heaviest snow amounts falling the northern Blue Mountains of Oregon and Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ski Bluewood resort in Columbia county Washington measured 15 inches of new snow over a 48 hour period ending at 0510am PST on Feb. 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow blanketed portions of the Spokane area early February 21st, due to a stalled front in the region. Snow continued through the morning and gradually dissipated after mid-day into early afternoon. The intensity and rate of snow fall was unexpected - only an inch was expected until the band of showers stalled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With numerous reports from broadcast media and the Washington Department of Transportation, early morning snowfall had a major impact on the commute due to the accumulations and icy roadways. Many accidents, rollovers, and slide offs occurred in the Spokane Metropolitan area. With icy roadways, numerous roads across hilly areas were closed, or unable to navigate, due to lack of traction. County and state officials were out plowing and deicing following the intense morning snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across the Columbia Basin, in Moses Lake, Quincy, and Ephrata, heavy snow began falling overnight that accumulated to 5 to 10 inches by mid-morning. Quincy School District cancelled school due to the poor weather conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell across northeastern Washington with an overnight, 12 hour accumulation of 4 to 5 inches in Newport, Kettle Falls, and Elk. Light snow continued off and on throughout the day with an additional 2 to 5 inches over the following 24 hours and ending around 0700PST on the 7th. Elk and Newport received a 48 hour total 7 to 10 inches of snow. There were several power outages due to the weather conditions on the 6th.

The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained strong winds and fresh snow created drifting snow and near zero visibility across the Waterville Plateau. There were numerous road closures and cars stranded on U.S. Highway 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a major winter storm that led to a state of emergency in the city of Leavenworth. Light snow early January 5th intensified overnight and continued into the early evening hours of the following day. Locations, such as Leavenworth, Coles Corner, and Plain, received 20 to 30 inches of snow in less than 24 hours. A two-day total snow accumulation of 27 inches was measured at Blewett Pass. The heavy snowfall led to numerous roof slides with widespread road closures and power outages. With the fresh snow, strong winds created blowing snow and snow drifts.

The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow across the upper Columbia Basin. Snow began around 2200PST bringing 4 to 6 inches of snow overnight in locations such as Ritzville, Odessa, and Davenport. Due to the heavy snow and slick road conditions, there was a slide off on Interstate 90 near Sprague. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow across the Spokane County. From midnight to 1600PST, there were 4 to 8 inches snow accumulation, with 5 inches measured at the Spokane International Airport. With rising daytime temperatures, the snow turned to sleet and freezing rain around 1600PST. There were 0.19 inches of ice measured at the airport from 1600 to 2000PST. Gusty winds, with a peak gust of 46 mph at the airport, led to tents collapsing at the county fair grounds and testing site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell in Republic, and surrounding areas, with an overnight 12 hour accumulation of 4 to 6 inches. Light snow continued off and on throughout the day with an additional 1 to 2 inches over the following 24 hours and ending around 0700PST on January 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft increased over the region as a Pacific low and a frontal boundary approached the Washington coast. Southerly winds increased, especially across |the Columbia Basin and Palouse on Sunday afternoon with gustiness continuing Sunday night. Snow spread into northeastern Washington on Sunday night with significant snowfall and increasing winds impacting the region on Monday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the late afternoon hours and began to accumulate around 1700PST. Snowfall rates picked up overnight and tapered off around 1000PST the following day. Heavy snow fell overnight with an accumulation of 6 to 12 inches down the Methow Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dumped 30 to 40 inches of snow across Stehekin and Stevens Pass over 48 hours. Large avalanches, with blowing and drifting snow, led to a seven day closure of US Highway 2 at Stevens Pass. There were more than 200 snow slides in Tumwater Canyon, east of the pass. Some slides measured 30 to 35 feet in height.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell in the Okanogan Valley, with an overnight, 12 hour accumulation of 5 to 11 inches in Okanogan, Malott, and Tonasket. Light snow continued off and on throughout the day with an additional 1 to 2 inches in parts of the valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow, with some freezing rain, across the Wenatchee area along the Columbia River. Snow accumulations were significant - 24 hour totals from 15 to 25 inches and 48 hour totals reaching 20 to 30 inches. There were reports of 2 inches of snow per hour for the first 12 hours of the storm. Near Chelan and Ardenvoir, there were numerous large snow slides leading to the closure of State Route 97. Blowing snow, due to strong winds, create poor visibility and difficult driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy, fresh snow, and freezing rain followed by strong winds to the Washington Palouse. There were 24 hour snow accumulations of 5 to 10 inches in Pullman, Colfax, and Oaksdale on January 6th. Blowing and drifting snow closed roads and led to accidents due to poor visibility and strong winds. A semi-truck rolled over on US Highway 195 shortly after the Pullman Airport and ASOS site reported a peak gust of 58 mph on the 7th. There was one minor injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eruption of Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'apai in Tonga on January 14th at 2027PST generated a tsunami that reach much of the Pacific rim, including the U.S. West coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tsunami generated by an eruption of the Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'api volcano reached the U.S. West coast.  The tsunami wave height of 0.5 feet was measured at the Westport tide gauge. No reports of damage or injury are known at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and persistent upper-level low-pressure system developed over the northern Rockies and southern Canada and then slowly migrated westward while continuously directing the jet stream and primary storm track at Washington State. This strong low-pressure system resulted in a 21-day period of a series of winter storms, wind storms, and atmospheric rivers that battered Washington State in rapid succession. First there was lowland snow the last week of December. Then a wind and rainstorm Jan 3 to 4. That was followed by two atmospheric river events from Jan 5 - 7 and Jan 11- 13 that resulted in heavy rain, minor to major flooding, and landslides. 2 flood fatalities occurred, and possibly a third from a landslide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt on saturated soils caused a landslide in this residential neighborhood, believed to have swept away or buried a man. The slide area is the size of multiple football fields and 15 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds peaked at 1200PST with a gust of 64 mph measured in Pomeroy. There were reports of downed trees and roofs damaged due to the strong winds.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of northwest flow over the Pacific Northwest guided several rounds of moisture advection across the mountain zones, leading to upslope moderate to heavy snow showers nearly each day. Meanwhile, surface frontal systems and a series of shortwave troughs in the flow aloft produced moderate to heavy snow as well as some periods of freezing rain across the lower elevations. Strong winds were also produced as a result of these shortwave troughs and surface frontal systems at the beginning and end of the prolonged winter weather conditions. During this extended period of winter weather, the I-84 and several state and US highways in Oregon and Washington closed for extended periods of time due to increased traffic accidents from ice and/or moderate to heavy snow accumulations on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer 17 miles NW of Roslyn, WA measured 17 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0700 PST on Jan. 3rd. A public report indicated 12 inches of new snow had fallen 12 miles NW of Tieton, WA at 1000 PST on Jan. 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of ice were present on roadways around Silverdale, WA at the end of January, which resulted in a fatal car accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was killed in a single car crash around 7:17 am PST Monday, 1/31/2022. According to Washington State Patrol, speed and ice were factors in the collision which resulted when a man in a truck attempted to speed past another vehicle, then lost control after hitting a patch of ice while on an overpass on Highway 303. The truck struck and rolled over a Jersey barrier, falling onto the road below. The driver died in the|crash, while a passenger sustained only minor injuries. Temperatures around the area were generally in the 30 to 33 degree range at the time of the incident and according to a media report icy roads ...were reported around the region with similar reports from Central Kitsap Fire and Rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and persistent upper-level low-pressure system developed over the northern Rockies and southern Canada and then slowly migrated westward while continuously directing the jet stream and primary storm track at Washington State. This strong low-pressure system resulted in a 21-day period of a series of winter storms, wind storms, and atmospheric rivers that battered Washington State in rapid succession. First there was lowland snow the last week of December. Then a wind and rainstorm Jan 3 to 4. That was followed by two atmospheric river events from Jan 5 - 7 and Jan 11- 13 that resulted in heavy rain, minor to major flooding, and landslides. 2 flood fatalities occurred, and possibly a third from a landslide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 6 to 10 inches, along with melting of remaining snow at lower elevations, lead to river flooding, significant urban and small stream flooding, and landslides. The peak of the rainfall was January 5 through January 7. Major flooding occurred on the Chehalis River and moderate flooding on the Satsop River. A motorist was stranded trying to cross a flooded road along the Chehalis River and was swept away when he left his vehicle. He was missing and presumed dead. Another man fatality occurred when an man tried to move his car out of flood danger and the car was swept away by flooding on the North River. Damages are reported and estimated; not all damages have been identified. Crop damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two strong Pacific fronts brought high winds to the coast as well as gusty winds to the Willamette Valley and interior lowlands of southwest Washington. This storm generated large seas impacting the coastline, and combined with rising river levels and high astronomical tides, resulted in some flooding along the coast due to poor drainage.  In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The worst flooding in a number of years (over a decade per the Emergency Manager) in the Tokeland area. Water level made it up to the Tokeland Hotel.  In the Raymond area, a 1/2 miles stretch of Highway 101 was closed, and 7 homes in the Peter St and Sherman Avenue reported flooding.  During this time high seas and high river levels in the area contributed to the flooding.  As a result, the tide peaked at 13.4 feet around 1230PST at the Tokeland tide gauge.

During this extended period of winter weather, the I-84 and several state and US highways in Oregon and Washington closed for extended periods of time due to increased traffic accidents from ice and/or moderate to heavy snow accumulations on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer 2 miles ENE of West Valley, WA measured 7.4 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0700 PST on Jan. 3rd. A trained spotter 3 miles E of Selah measured 5 inches of new snow at 1000 PST on Jan. 3rd.

Meanwhile, surface frontal systems and a series of shortwave troughs in the flow aloft produced moderate to heavy snow as well as some periods of freezing rain across the lower elevations. Strong winds were also produced as a result of these shortwave troughs and surface frontal systems at the beginning and end of the prolonged winter weather conditions. During this extended period of winter weather, the I-84 and several state and US highways in Oregon and Washington closed for extended periods of time due to increased traffic accidents from ice and/or moderate to heavy snow accumulations on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cocorahs observer WA-KL-16 1 mile NW of White Salmon, WA measured 15 inches of new snow at 0700 PST on Jan. 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two strong Pacific fronts brought high winds to the coast as well as gusty winds to the Willamette Valley and interior lowlands of southwest Washington. This storm generated large seas impacting the coastline, and combined with rising river levels and high astronomical tides, resulted in some flooding along the coast due to poor drainage.  In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very active frontal system brought heavy snow to the Columbia River Gorge on the 3rd with snow totals between 12 and 22 inches.  This resulted in the closure of SR-14 which connects western and eastern Oregon and Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and persistent upper-level low-pressure system developed over the northern Rockies and southern Canada and then slowly migrated westward while continuously directing the jet stream and primary storm track at Washington State. This strong low-pressure system resulted in a 21-day period of a series of winter storms, wind storms, and atmospheric rivers that battered Washington State in rapid succession. First there was lowland snow the last week of December. Then a wind and rainstorm Jan 3 to 4. That was followed by two atmospheric river events from Jan 5 - 7 and Jan 11- 13 that resulted in heavy rain, minor to major flooding, and landslides. 2 flood fatalities occurred, and possibly a third from a landslide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on top of saturated soils triggered a landslide that knocked a house off it's foundation, collapsing one level - destroying it and trapping the inhabitants. One man was trapped under debris and injured and had to be extricated by Seattle Fire Department. A woman was also pinned under debris but was able to escape. One dog was killed and another was found a week later in the debris and extricated. At the time, 1 to 2 inches of rain had fallen in the area in the previous 48 hours with the Seattle Antecedent Rainfall Index already high, above the threshold for heavy rain-induced landslides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft increased over the region as a Pacific low and a frontal boundary approached the Washington coast. Southerly winds increased, especially across |the Columbia Basin and Palouse on Sunday afternoon with gustiness continuing Sunday night. Snow spread into northeastern Washington on Sunday night with significant snowfall and increasing winds impacting the region on Monday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 2 was closed at Stevens Pass for avalanche control at 0500PST. There was additional closure due to spin outs, blowing and drifting snow, and strong winds. Stevens Pass recorded 12 inches of snow accumulation in the morning of January 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft increased over the region as a Pacific low and a frontal boundary approached the Washington coast. Southerly winds increased, especially across |the Columbia Basin and Palouse on Sunday afternoon with gustiness continuing Sunday night. Snow spread into northeastern Washington on Sunday night with significant snowfall and increasing winds impacting the region on Monday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the early evening hours and began to accumulate around 1900PST. Snowfall rates picked up overnight and tapered off around 1600PST the following day. Heavy snow fell overnight with an accumulation of 7 to 11 inches across the Okanogan Highlands, including 6.5 inches at Sherman Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft increased over the region as a Pacific low and a frontal boundary approached the Washington coast. Southerly winds increased, especially across |the Columbia Basin and Palouse on Sunday afternoon with gustiness continuing Sunday night. Snow spread into northeastern Washington on Sunday night with significant snowfall and increasing winds impacting the region on Monday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the early evening hours and continued off and on over the next 24 hours. Snowfall rates picked up overnight with an accumulation of 6 to 12 inches across the northeast mountains. 49 North Ski Resort recorded 10 inches at the base in the morning of January 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the early evening hours on January 5th and heavy snow began to accumulate around midnight. Snowfall rates picked up overnight with an accumulation of 15 to 23 inches across the Methow Valley, including Twisp, Winthrop, and Carlton, by the morning. Snow began to taper off that evening with Twisp receiving a 36 hour snow accumulation of 30 inches ending 0700PST January 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two strong Pacific fronts brought high winds to the coast as well as gusty winds to the Willamette Valley and interior lowlands of southwest Washington. This storm generated large seas impacting the coastline, and combined with rising river levels and high astronomical tides, resulted in some flooding along the coast due to poor drainage.  In addition to the wind and rain, heavy snow fell in the mountains and in the Columbia River Gorge where  transportation corridors between western and eastern Oregon and Washington were temporarily closed due the heavy snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds were reported along the south Washington coast with sustained winds between 35 and 55 mph with a peak gust to 69 mph at Cape Disappointment (82SW1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a series of snow events in the lowlands and mountains of western Washington, a system off the Pacific brought a modifying airmass towards the area right before the Christmas holiday. Warmer Pacific air was able to overrun lingering cold air very near the surface, producing a warm layer of classic characteristic of a freezing rain profile. As such, measurable ice accumulation led to hazardous impacts across the area, most noticeably to roadways and travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: First reports of freezing rain were provided by spotters on the night of the 22nd, where multiple collisions were the result from freezing rain along I-5. It was also reported that ice was beginning to coat surfaces in Chehalis late that evening. By 4am on the 23rd, reports of 0.2 inches of ice were received out of Tumwater and then by 10am, reports from around Olympia measured around 0.25 inches of ice. Lacey, nearby to Olympia received around 0.23 inches of ice.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low coupled with cold front at the surface brought numerous wind and winter weather impacts across portions of eastern Washington and eastern Oregon. Moisture rounding the upper low produced heavy snow across portions of the east slopes of the Cascades, while the cold front produced strong winds in the lower elevations during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Goose Prairie, WA measured 12 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period, and 18 inches of new snow in 48 hour period ending at 2200 PDT. The Washington Dept. of Highways at a point 10 miles west of Rimrock, WA measured 18 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0500 PDT on April 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low coupled with cold front at the surface brought numerous wind and winter weather impacts across portions of eastern Washington and eastern Oregon. Moisture rounding the upper low produced heavy snow across portions of the east slopes of the Cascades, while the cold front produced strong winds in the lower elevations during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station EW5837 Kahlotus 1 mile southwest of Kahlotus, WA measured a 70 mph wind gust at 0930 PDT. The mesonet station DW9572 Benton City 4 miles northwest of West Richland, WA measured a 62 mph gust at 0930 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 7:00 PM on the evening of April 18th, a weak waterspout was reported on the eastern shore of Lake Samish (near Bellingham) in Whatcom County, Washington. Video evidence confirmed the waterspout. This waterspout resulted in damage to three boats docked on the shore of Lake Samish and other minor damages. This event will be classified as a waterspout and not a tornado given the rapid dissipation of the waterspout as it came onshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WATERSPOUT. Location 48.661, -122.374. At 7:00 PM on the evening of April 18th, a weak waterspout was reported on the eastern shore of Lake Samish (near Bellingham) in Whatcom County, Washington. Video evidence confirmed the waterspout. This waterspout resulted in damage to three boats docked on the shore of Lake Samish and other minor damages. This event will be classified as a waterspout and not a tornado given the rapid dissipation of the waterspout as it came onshore.

Snow began during the very late evening hours of the 3rd and picked up intensity throughout the day on April 4th and 5th before finally tapering off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early-spring storm system affected Western Washington, bringing very heavy snow to the Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began to fall around midnight on April 4th across the Central Cascades and quickly accumulated. Within a 12 hour period from the onset, around 12 inches of new snow was reported at Steven Pass. Snow continued to be heaviest throughout the day on April 4th and into the early morning hours on the 5th. Snow then finally tapered later that evening.||Snow also accumulated at Snoqualmie Pass during this same timeframe but it did not meet or exceed winter storm warning thresholds. Overall snow totals for this multi-day event was around 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cooler and wetter than normal conditions continued towards mid April as weather systems continued to move into Western Washington from off the Pacific. On April 9th, heavy snow was reported at Snoqualmie Pass, with then an additional system moving into Oregon bringing lowland snow to areas south of Olympia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow affected Snoqualmie Pass April 9th through the 10th. Heavy snow began during the late morning hours on the 9th and continued through the afternoon on the 10th. Around 18 inches of new snow was reported at the Pass within a 24 hour period. Snow then tapered by the evening of the 10th.

On April 9th, heavy snow was reported at Snoqualmie Pass, with then an additional system moving into Oregon bringing lowland snow to areas south of Olympia.

A strong storm system moving into Oregon carried enough moisture north of it to bring accumulating snow to portions of the Lower Chehalis Valley. Areas of the city of Chehalis reported around 2 to 3 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong storm system brought a prolonged period of strong winds to Western Washington in early April. This same storm system also led to high surf conditions near the Coast and heavy snow in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The DW7371 CWOP on Lopez Island in San Juan County reported sustained wind of 42 mph with a gust of 60 mph around midnight on April 4th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The FW4667 CWOP station near Port Townsend in Jefferson County reported sustained winds around 50 mph with several gusts to 63 mph late in the evening on April 4th (around 2300L).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 11th, a strong storm moved the area and brought heavy snow to the Blewett Pass area. It impacted travel through the area. The Pass received a two day total of 18 inches by the morning of April 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amount of 10 inches accumulated between 0200 to 1300LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 11th, a strong storm moved the area and brought heavy snow to the Blewett Pass area. It impacted travel through the area. The Pass received a two day total of 18 inches by the morning of April 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amount of 8 inches accumulated between 4/11 1300 to 4/12 1300LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder air mass with a low pressure system moved through the region bringing another round of snow to the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started on late the 26th and started to accumulate early on the 27th into the 28th. About 15 to 17 inches of new snow fell throughout a 12 hour period - where a winter weather advisory was issued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front brought 24 to 36 hours of moderate to heavy snow to the Cascades. A strong storm arrived late April 3rd and remained through the 4th, impacting highway winter travel and local power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 2 at Stevens Pass recorded 23.5 inches of snow accumulation over almost 36 hour time period. There were high winds, with gusts up to 58 mph, that led to blowing snow and poor visibilities. The Stevens Pass Ski Resort was closed on April 4th due to power outages from the weather conditions and avalanche mitigation work.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms developed to the west of Spokane during the early evening hours of 6 May 2022.  As they approached the west side of the metropolitan area, the storms became more organized.  The strongest cell developed rotation periodically as it moved from the Airway Heights area eastward through Spokane and Spokane Valley.  Two distinct areas of damage occurred due to tornadoes as the storm moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado followed a due west-to-east path along W. Greenwood Road. The damage indicators along the road mainly consisted of trees that were uprooted or snapped. Several powerline poles were brought down by fallen trees toward the middle of the damage path. No substantial damage was noted to structures along the path. A video that was shared by a resident near N. Kynan Rd. showed a very weak circulation moving due east along W. Greenwood Rd. This video footage confirms the timing and width of the tornado. Residents in the vicinity of Russell Rd. confirmed no further damage beyond this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms developed to the west of Spokane during the early evening hours of 6 May 2022.  As they approached the west side of the metropolitan area, the storms became more organized.  The strongest cell developed rotation periodically as it moved from the Airway Heights area eastward through Spokane and Spokane Valley.  Two distinct areas of damage occurred due to tornadoes as the storm moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado generated a discontinuous line of damage first observed near the intersection of E. 9th Avenue and S. Cuba St. Video footage confirms a funnel cloud lowered at times and was disorganized as it moved from west to east. A second area of more significant damage was observed along 6th Avenue that included roof damage and many trees uprooted or snapped at the base. The third and longest track of damage began near E. 5th Ave and S. McKinnon Rd and included significant damage to trees and unsecured items along E 4th Avenue. The most noteworthy damage along E. 4th Ave was to a line of travel trailers, vehicles, and boats, several of which were tossed or displaced. Numerous large ponderosa pine trees were uprooted or snapped along E. 4th Avenue before the tornado again lifted. The final isolated area of damage was located along E. 5th Avenue between S. Coleman Rd and S Grand Rd. Security camera footage confirmed brief circulation at the surface along with two downed trees along the southern edge of the track before the tornado lifted for the final time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold core funnel cloud formed in weak convective cells 5 miles east of Pullman WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small funnel cloud dissipated shortly after formation. There is no indication that the circulation ever made contact with the ground. The location is estimated from social media shared by the media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Parks Wildfire on August 21. It was added to the NW Pasayten Complex on September 6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parks Fire started on August 21 by lightning. It became part of the NW Pasayten Complex on September 6. It is located 30 miles NE of Mazama in rugged timber terrain. It burned 20759 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough combined with instability allowed convection to develop in the afternoon. One traffic fatality on Interstate 5 near Kelso, Washington is attributed to hail accumulating on highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington State Patrol reported all lanes of I5 closed around exit 42 due to traffic accidents attributed to hail accumulating on pavement. Three to five vehicles involved in accidents. There was one fatality and one injury. Note: Hail size estimated from other reports in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic frontal boundary pushed through Western Washington, bringing wintry precipitation in the form of snow and colder weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS reports 4.8 inches of snowfall in Indianola, with 3.9 inches just south of Indianola. About 2.5 inches of snow was reported 2.8 miles east of Bremerton. A report of 5.9 inches was received 0.9 miles South of Seabeck - while another spotter report of 7.5 inches was reported in Seabeck. At 11am, spotter (kitsap 58) reported 9.6 inches of snow has fallen since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front passage on May 18th produced breezy to gusty winds across the forecast area, with strong winds mainly being reported across central and southeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station EW5837 Kahlotus which is 1 mile southwest of Kahlotus, WA measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph at 1230 PST. The mesonet station HRMTN 10.1 NW Benton City, WA measured  wind gust of 59 mph at 1430 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region on May 18. Wind  gusts acorss the region ranged from 40 to 50 MPH. Several reports of minor tree damage and shingles blown off roofs across the Inland Northwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Tree Fell Onto A Moving Vehicle On Malaga-Alcoa Highway. No Injuries Were Reported. Traffic On The Highway Was Halted For Around An Hour. Time Is Estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region on May 18. Wind  gusts acorss the region ranged from 40 to 50 MPH. Several reports of minor tree damage and shingles blown off roofs across the Inland Northwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half Of A Mobile Home Blew Off A Trailer On State Route 206 Near Bruce Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region on May 18. Wind  gusts acorss the region ranged from 40 to 50 MPH. Several reports of minor tree damage and shingles blown off roofs across the Inland Northwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees Down Blocking Both Lanes Of Sr 21 In Ferry County At Milepost 183 At Goosmus Creek Road. This Report Was From Wsdot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region on May 18. Wind  gusts acorss the region ranged from 40 to 50 MPH. Several reports of minor tree damage and shingles blown off roofs across the Inland Northwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees Blocking Northbound Lanes Of Washington State Road 25 At Mile Post 104 (Between Kettle Falls And Northport). This Report Was From Wsdot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold air was in place over the Pacific Northwest, with temperatures marginally cold enough for a low elevation snow event from the Portland metro northward. A storm system moved over northwest Oregon and southwest Washington bringing widespread snowfall to many areas north of Salem, Oregon. This system brought several inches of snow to the Willamette Valley in addition to the Coast Range and Cascades. By the late morning/early afternoon, much of the snow west of the Columbia River Gorge had transitioned to rain. This was not just your standard snowfall as it ended up being the first measurable lowland snowfall in the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 8 to 14 inches of snow fell, most of which fell within a 12 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold air was in place over the Pacific Northwest, with temperatures marginally cold enough for a low elevation snow event from the Portland metro northward. This system brought several inches of snow to the Willamette Valley in addition to the Coast Range and Cascades. CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough pushed several low pressure systems across the Pacific Northwest in the middle of April.  The trough allowed for a much colder than normal air mass to settle into portions of eastern Washington and Oregon, allowing snow levels to drop to the surface across a majority of these areas. This ultimately resulted in snow accumulations in the mountains and lower elevations, with significant snow accumulations being observed in some mountain zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer 4 miles northeast of White Salmon, WA measured 12 inches of new snow overnight at 0830 PDT. A Co-Op observer 1 mile south of Snowden, WA measured 12.6 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 1102 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of wintry weather was noted across the Western Washington lowlands at the beginning of December, earlier than what is typically experienced. Snow began at the end of November with several rounds continuing through the first few days of September. While accumulations did not meet winter storm criteria Dec 1-2, widespread heavy snow accumulations were experienced on Dec 3. These conditions were largely due to Fraser outflow in the low levels. with deep upper troughing in the upper levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow continued at times through the first few days of December. Observations averaged less than 1 inch on Dec 1, with an average of 1 inch on Dec 2 and an average of 2-3 inches of new snow on Dec 3 (highest amounts of 4-5 inches reported, especially from Mill Creek to Everett).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level ridge of high pressure moved over the region and persisted through the end of July and the first of August. Across the region, multiple areas in the lower elevations reached critical thresholds for heat risk, while many mountain zones saw consecutive days with high temperatures exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calculated heat risk values at the Dallesport Airport GHCN site recorded consecutive days between July 26th through Aug. 1st of temperatures that met or exceeded excessive heat warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding between 8 AM - 11 AM high tide cycle on 12/27. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of above normal temperatures and heat continued to affect portions of Western Washington at the end of August. CZ_NAME: EASTERN STRAIT OF JUAN DE FUCA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of wintry weather was noted across the Western Washington lowlands at the beginning of December, earlier than what is typically experienced. Snow began at the end of November with several rounds continuing through the first few days of September. While accumulations did not meet winter storm criteria Dec 1-2, widespread heavy snow accumulations were experienced on Dec 3. These conditions were largely due to Fraser outflow in the low levels. with deep upper troughing in the upper levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level ridge of high pressure moved over the region and persisted through the end of July and the first of August. Across the region, multiple areas in the lower elevations reached critical thresholds for heat risk, while many mountain zones saw consecutive days with high temperatures exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calculated heat risk values at the Yakima Airport GHCN site recorded several days between July 27th through the 31st of temperatures that met or exceeded excessive heat warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure built across the Pacific Northwest at the end of July, making for well-above normal temperatures for most of Western Washington. Afternoon high temperatures in the interior lowlands soared into the 90s, with little relief during the overnight periods with lows in the 60s. This brought about a high probability of heat-related impacts to the area, especially those without an adequate means of cooling and/or hydration. Towards the beginning of August, the return to onshore flow from the Pacific brought about a relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot weather affected the Northern Interior of Western Washington. A record high temperature of 90 was set at the Bellingham airport the afternoon of July 26. This broke the previous record of 86 degrees set back in 1971 and 1988.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern brought several rounds of snow to the Olympic and Cascade mountains of Western Washington. The heaviest snow fell across the central and southern Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began at Stevens Pass during the early afternoon hours of the 3rd, with around 12 inches of new snow accumulating within a 12 hours period shortly after midnight on the 4th. Snow then continued through most of the morning hours on the 4th before temperatures rose above freezing. ||At Snoqualmie Pass, there was a burst of heavy snow on the 3rd, similar to the conditions at Stevens Pass initially. Amounts fell just below winter weather advisory levels, with about 10 inches falling within a 12 hour period. Snow continued into the 4th before temperatures rose above freezing, with little additional accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern brought several rounds of snow to the Olympic and Cascade mountains of Western Washington. The heaviest snow fell across the central and southern Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Decent snowfall began at Mt Rainier National Park on the 3rd in the early afternoon. New snow accumulation recorded at Paradise suggested about 11 inches of new snow fell within a 12 hour period. Snow then continued through the 4th before tapering into the early hours of the 6th. By daybreak on the 6th, about 14 inches of new snow had accumulated at the Park.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy snow affected the Cascade and Olympic mountains of western Washington at the end of November. Similar to the previous event earlier in the month, the central and southern Cascades received the most snowfall during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began at Snoqualmie Pass during the late morning hours on the 29th, quickly accumulating 12 inches within 12 hours and over 20 inches in 24 hours. By the end of the month during this event, a final snow depth at the Pass reached 35 inches. Decent snowfall still continued into December, with the bulk of the accumulation during the 29/30th period of November. ||For Stevens Pass, decent snowfall fell on the 27th and 28th but did not reach advisory or warning criteria. Then, on Nov 30th, a period of heavy snow brought accumulations of 12 inches within a 12 hours period to the Pass. Snow initially began around noon on the 29th, with snow becoming heavier the night of the 29th into the 30th. Snow then tapered into the early morning hours of December 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy snow affected the Cascade and Olympic mountains of western Washington at the end of November. Similar to the previous event earlier in the month, the central and southern Cascades received the most snowfall during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of decent snowfall fell at Mt Rainier National Park shortly after midnight on the 27th. Snow remained steady through the 28th but did not meet advisory or warning criteria, with about 15 inches of new snow depth through this period. Much heavier snow then fell on the 29th/30th. Snow began during the mid morning hours on the 29th, accumulating well over 11 inches in 12 hours and over 20 inches in 24 hours. Heavy snow then tapered during the late morning hours on the 30th before starting up again later in day but remaining generally less impactful. New snow depth after this storm at Paradise was measured around 2 feet, making for about 70 inches of total snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final week of July a heat wave struck the Inland NW with an abundance of sunshine each day. High temperatures warmed into the 90s from the 24th through the 26th and|then upper 90s and triple digits from the 27th through the 31st. Temperatures across the region ranged from 112 to 96. Spokane Felts Field was one of the hottest locations in the area, reaching 105F on the 31st. Spokane airport achieved 100 degree or warmer four consecutive days, which tied for 2nd place for the longest consecutive 100+ degree day streak on record.  Thirteen daily records were broken across the Inland Northwest. DOH reported heat caused 224 emergency patients in Eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperature for the day was 110. An 88-year-old resident of a Wenatchee assisted-living facility went outside and fell on July 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the region and northerly winds aloft pushed a cold airmass into the region, with the northern valley zones feeling the impacts by the morning of November 2nd. This airmass infiltration resulted in areas of the Yakima and Kittitas valleys experiencing morning temperatures at or below the freezing mark for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington DOT station TRYEG Ryegrass Summit recorded temperatures at or below freezing between 0200PST and 0800 PST. The mesonet station EW4750 Ellensburg recorded temperatures at or below freezing between 0200 PST and 0745 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two bands of heavy rain impacted Okanogan county during the afternoon. Areas impacted included Northeast of Mazama and Conconully. Rainfall ranged from 0.5 to 0.9 for Mazama and 0.6 to 1.4 for Conconully. The burn scars of Cedar Creek and Muckamuck from 2021 were in the flood areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Evacuations ongoing along the north fork Salmon Creek road, with the bridge on north side of town overtopped with water. Several people are trapped in Kerr campground from flash flooding of the north fork of the Salmon river. North fork salmon creek road washed out in multiple locations from flash flooding. Sinlahekin road flooded and impassable. Road washed out near Salmon Meadows campground from flash flooding. |Reports of at least 25 residences inundated with water. A cabin under construction was destroyed from flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough slid down the coast of British Columbia facilitating a period of strong southerly winds along Blue Mountains foothills, and heavy snow across the mountain areas between November 29th and December 1st. Wind gusts of 60-65mph were measured along the foothills of the northern Blue Mountains and snow accumulations of up to 22 inches were reported in the Washington Cascades, and up to 24 inches in portions of the northern Blue Mountains of Oregon and Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Employee 9 miles north of White Salmon reported 14 inches of new snow at 0440 on Nov. 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down, blocking roads or on powerlines in Washougal and Brush Prairie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level ridging over the area brought unseasonably warm and dry conditions over a extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record high temperature at Hoquiam Airport of 76, breaking the record of 74 in 1961.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm produced 1 hail near Colfax, WA.
The Nilles wildfire started at 6:30AM by unknown cause. It burned 500 acres before being contained around 4:30PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nilles fire burned 500 acres of grassland. No structures damaged or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm passed South of Chelan, WA and started the Stayman wildfire. The fired burned around 1200 acres and lasted from July 18 to July 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stayman Fire was first reported on July 18 at approximately 2:35 p.m. The fire is burned in brush and grass. It was first assessed at 35 acres and grew to approximately 1200 acres.  Level 1 evacuations were in place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge brought record heat area wide in late September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record high temperature of 82 was set at Seattle-Tacoma WA airport on September 26th. This breaks the old record of 78 set in 1970.||A record high temperature of 80 was set at Seattle WA WFO on September 26th. This breaks the old record of 79 set in 1949.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fallen tree at Pratt Park in the Central District per photo in social media (Twitter) this aligns with the wind advisory that we had out for the area on Friday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river pushed heavy rain and gusty winds throughout Western Washington causing numerous damage reports as wind advisories and high wind warnings were issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report on social media is as follows:||Vehicle high centered on a tree that fell across Lundeen Park Way just west of highway 9 in Lake Stevens. Driver is uninjured. There was a High Wind Warning that was issued for this location with gusts up to 55 to 60 mph expected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Siouxon Fire started in rather benign weather conditions with seasonal temperatures.  The fire cause of the fire was determined to be human caused - an abandoned camp fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Siouxon Fire, caused by an abandoned campfire, was reported on the 22nd. It grew to around 306 acres by the end of the month. The fire was primarily in the Siouxon Creek drainage in the southwest corner of the Gifford Pinchot National Forest, in remote hard to reach area which was previously impacted by the 2020 Big Hollow Fire and is uncontained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the region during the afternoon on November 4th into the morning hours of November 5th accompanied by a strong low level jet and tight surface pressure gradient. The strongest winds occurred late on the evening of November 4th and on the early morning of November 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station KRLD measured a gust of 63 mph at 0015PDT on November 5th and the mesonet station XKEL measured a gust of 59mph at 0047PDT on November 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter snow brought heavy snow to portions of the lowlands. Deep upper level troughing along with Fraser outflow near the surface supplied the proper set up for winter weather. Dynamic cooling also played a major role in areas of heaviest snow, especially around Kitsap, King, and Snohomish Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to portions of the Kitsap Peninsula at the end of November. Areas around Bremerton generally received less than 1 inch of new snow, while neighboring areas on the peninsula received around 7 inches of snow, for instance, Seabeck. Areas around Poulsbo received around 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the region during the afternoon on November 4th into the morning hours of November 5th accompanied by a strong low level jet and tight surface pressure gradient. The strongest winds occurred late on the evening of November 4th and on the early morning of November 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station CW0988 measured a gust of 60mph at 2330PDT November 4th. In addition, Benton PUD(via New Article) reported more than 2,100 customers without power by 11 p.m. Friday November. This was confirmed by members of the Public via facebook. Another member of the public posted a  picture to facebook of a moderately sized tree snapped in half in Prosser Washington and reported trees down, roads blocked suggesting High Wind Warning magnitude wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter snow brought heavy snow to portions of the lowlands. Deep upper level troughing along with Fraser outflow near the surface supplied the proper set up for winter weather. Dynamic cooling also played a major role in areas of heaviest snow, especially around Kitsap, King, and Snohomish Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to portions of Western Skagit County at the end of November. While most areas at lowest elevation received less than an inch of snow, locations such as Lyman received about 5 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system started to impact the region on November 4 and lasted through November 7. It brought early season widespread snow and high winds to Eastern Washington and the Idaho Panhandle. Cocorahs observers reported 13 to 20 inches of snow for the Cascades, 14 to 25 inches for the Northern portions of the Okanogan Highlands,  and 4 to 10 inches for the through the Spokane Metro area for the 4-7 of Nov.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Leavenworth had an estimated 6 inches between 0000-1000LST Nov 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary thunderstorm stayed over the area for several hours and produced significant amount of rain over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain induced landslide reported near Dead Horse Point on nf-5400. There were no observations directly in the in area. The observations in the general area showed amounts of half an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the White River Wildfire in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the White River Fire. It started burning August 11 and lasted through the end of the month. It is burning timber in rugged wilderness. No structures lost. It has burned 5640 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Irving Peak Wildfire in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Irving Fire. It started burning August 11 and lasted through the end of the month. It is burning timber in rugged wilderness. No structures lost. It has burned 5639 acres. The fire has been combined with the White River Fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed Low over the region shifted over North Idaho and increased instability over the area. The instability mixed with afternoon convection to develop isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A collapsing thunderstorm brought outflow winds downing trees and power lines West of Springdale, Southeast of Tum Tum, and Northeast of Loon Lake. 1200 customers were without power for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northerly pressure gradient after the passage of an artic frontal boundary facilitated a period of extremely cold to hazardous wind chills, focused the evening of December 21st through the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS station KELN recorded wind chill values at or below -20 degrees from 6pm December 21st to 2am December 22nd, and the Mesonet station TRYEG recorded wind chill values at or below -20 degrees from 12AM to 3AM on December 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northerly pressure gradient after the passage of an artic frontal boundary facilitated a period of extremely cold to hazardous wind chills, focused the evening of December 21st through the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station THORS recorded wind chill values at or below -20 degrees from 2AM to 11AM December 22nd and the Mesonet station AV456 recorded wind chill values at or below -20 degrees from 4AM to 11AM December 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the slow moving Low continued through the region, another round of thunderstorms over the Eastern Washington produced large hail and strong winds to the region. The storms impacted extreme Eastern WA and the North ID Panhandle. Thunderstorms developed along an axis of instability stretching along the border between WA and ID.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm dropped hail ranging from half an inch to 2.75. Most hail reports ranged from 0.75 to 1.5. A 2.75 size was reported around Newman Lake at 1700PST. It traveled from Albion WA to Newman Lake WA around 1525PST to 1715PST. It damaged at least 15000 acres of crops, broke windows and damaged siding of homes in Liberty Lake, and dented numerous vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the slow moving Low continued through the region, another round of thunderstorms over the Eastern Washington produced large hail and strong winds to the region. The storms impacted extreme Eastern WA and the North ID Panhandle. Thunderstorms developed along an axis of instability stretching along the border between WA and ID.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds moved North from Oakesdale WA to Rockford WA. A weather station clocked gusts to 60 mph. It damaged or knocked down power lines and over 100 trees. It caused power outages around 3200 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough slid down the coast of British Columbia facilitating a period of strong southerly winds along Blue Mountains foothills, and heavy snow across the mountain areas between November 29th and December 1st.
