EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ski resort Ski Bluewood measured 12 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0500 PST on Dec. 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of deformation bands aided by a closed upper low off the southwest Oregon coastline resulted in light to moderate snowfall through much of the day Dec. 4th and through the morning on Dec. 5th. The prolonged snowfall resulted in heavy snow accumulations across the Lower Columbia Basin, portions of the eastern Columbia River Gorge, the Blue Mountain Foothills, and in the Yakima valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Co-Op observer 1 mile north northwest of Sunnyside, Wa measured 5.5 inches of new over a 24 hour period ending at 0816 PST on Dec. 5th. A Cocorahs observer 2 miles north northeast of Granger, Wa measured 4 inches of new snow over a 24 hour period ending at 0930 PST on Dec. 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed Low over the region shifted over North Idaho and increased instability over the area. The instability mixed with afternoon convection to develop isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed  in North Spokane and produced large hail. The hail size ranged from half an inch to one and quarter inch. Picture relayed to television media of hailstones estimated to be between the size of pennies and half dollars. Time estimated from radar at 1712PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed Low over the region shifted over North Idaho and increased instability over the area. The instability mixed with afternoon convection to develop isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Collapsing thunderstorms caused downed tree limbs and numerous power outages near Chewelah and areas to the southwest. Estimated over 2000 people were without power. Time estimated from radar. No observed gusts were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of deformation bands aided by a closed upper low off the southwest Oregon coastline resulted in light to moderate snowfall through much of the day Dec. 4th and through the morning on Dec. 5th. The prolonged snowfall resulted in heavy snow accumulations across the Lower Columbia Basin, portions of the eastern Columbia River Gorge, the Blue Mountain Foothills, and in the Yakima valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media post in Richland, Wa indicated 4 inches of new snow accumulations at 0347 PST on Dec. 5th. A Co-Op observer 8 miles north northeast of Hermiston, Or measured 4.8 inches of new snow over a 24 hour period ending at 0705 PST on Dec. 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of deformation bands aided by a closed upper low off the southwest Oregon coastline resulted in light to moderate snowfall through much of the day Dec. 4th and through the morning on Dec. 5th. The prolonged snowfall resulted in heavy snow accumulations across the Lower Columbia Basin, portions of the eastern Columbia River Gorge, the Blue Mountain Foothills, and in the Yakima valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report from Walla Walla, Wa indicated 4 inches of new snow accumulated at 0340 PST on Dec. 5th. A trained spotter 4 miles west of College Place, Wa measured 4 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0841 PST on Dec. 5th. A social media report 1 mile east northeast of Walla Walla, Wa indicated 4.3 inches of new snow accumulated in a 24 hour period ending at 1031 PST on Dec. 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: AN upper level trough and surface frontal passage resulted in heavy snow occurring in the higher terrain of the Eastern Columbia River Gorge, near the east slopes of the Cascade mountain range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 1 mile west of White Salmon, WA measured 4 inches of new snow at 1800 PST on Dec. 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level trough impacting the Pacific Northwest placed the region under a moist southwest flow aloft, and combined with a strong cold air pooling along the eastern slopes of the Washington Cascades, resulted in heavy snow occurring in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 2 miles northwest of Yakima, Wa measured 5.8 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0835 PST on Dec. 10th. Via a public report from Tieton, Wa, 5 inches of new snow was measured at 0550 PST on Dec. 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fire started when a combine caught fire at the intersection of Ridpath and Little Goose Dam Roads South of Hay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combine caught fire South of Hay. The fire spread into nearby grass and grew to 5680 acres. It burned from the afternoon of August 4 until August 7. The driver of the combine was injured. No structures lost.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level trough impacting the Pacific Northwest placed the region under a moist southwest flow aloft, and combined with a strong cold air pooling along the eastern slopes of the Washington Cascades, resulted in heavy snow occurring in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer 1 mile south southwest of Trout Lake, WA measured 17.5 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0730 PST on Dec. 10th. A trained spotter 22 miles N of White Salmon, WA measured 12.5 inches of new snow at 2135 Dec. 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of surface frontal boundaries, warm air overrunning low level and valley cold pools, and a moderate transport of moisture in a west to southwest flow aloft resulted in impactful wintry conditions across southeast Washington, northeast and central Oregon. A mix of heavy freezing rain and heavy snow resulted in numerous traffic accidents and highway closures across the region during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 1 mile west of White Salmon, WA measured 4 inches of new snow at 1313 PST on Dec. 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air aloft and cold pooling in valley areas combined with an upper trough influencing the regions weather resulted in freezing rain developing across portions of the east slopes of the Washington Cascades and portions of southeastern and south central Washington over Dec. 26th and 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Co-Op observer in Sunnyside, WA measured 0.25 inches of ice accumulation from freezing rain from the previous evening at 0200 PST on Dec. 27th. Via a public report from Granger, WA, 0.25 inches of ice accumulation from freezing rain was reported at 0200 PST on Dec. 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the WA coast continued to push weak waves of instability across Eastern WA. The instability was able to produce mid morning thunderstorms with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm formed near Malden and moved Northeast near Plaza. It produced hail the size of half an inch to 1.5 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the WA coast continued to push weak waves of instability across Eastern WA. The instability was able to produce mid morning thunderstorms with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed East of Connell and traveled North toward Ritzville. It produced  hail 1.5 inch in Lind WA around 0709PST. It also had reports of 0.75 inch and 1 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the WA coast continued to push weak waves of instability across Eastern WA. The instability was able to produce mid morning thunderstorms with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed near Havillah and traveled Northeast into Canada. Public reported 1 inch hail near Chesaw, WA at 1730PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Low brought several rounds of thunderstorms to the Eastern Washington. The thunderstorms produced large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding to the region. The first round impacted Southeast WA. The Low increased instability and low level moisture leading to decent afternoon convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud and debris over highway 12 from heavy rain from a thunderstorm. One lane closed  and police had to help direct traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Low brought several rounds of thunderstorms to the Eastern Washington. The thunderstorms produced large hail, strong winds, and flash flooding to the region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches of trees were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fire started from farm equipment working and field and quickly spread in the dry grasses by 25 MPH winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farm equipment working a field sparked a wildfire that was quickly spread through grass fields by winds. It burned from August 4-6. It burned 2500 acres. Six homes and eight outbuildings were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty North winds behind a cold front funneled down the Okanogan Valley and over the western portion of the Columbia Basin. Speeds reached into the 35 to 40 MPH. A long dry spell allowed the winds to pick up loose soil and dust from a recently worked farmland. It caused reduced visibility across the area and led collisions and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 2 between mileposts 198 and 200 was closed due to reduced visibility from dust storm. Several collisions were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms brought heavy rain to the Cedar Creek Burn Scar area creating a debris flow across Highway 20. Observations in the near the site in Mazama showed rain amounts half an inch in hour and three quarters of inch in 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Cedar Creek Burn Scar caused a debris flow over Highway 20 near Mazama. Observations in the near the site in Mazama showed precip amounts half an inch in hour and three quarters of inch in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low brought scattered thunderstorms to the region. Most thunderstorms carried gusty winds and small hail. One thunderstorm recorded one inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm recorded one inch hail. It formed South of Lake Omak around 1835PST and moved North toward Omak. It lasted until 1940PST. Time is estimated by radar. No damage or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system off the coast of British Columbia placed the region under a moist southwest flow aloft. At the lower levels, this flow, already saturated soils, and increasing instability in the afternoon allowed developing storms to produce high rainfall rates. Areas of Columbia county, WA saw these high rain rates, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 6 miles east of Dayton, WA reported that 2.5 inches of rainfall had fallen in less than an hour. This then led to runoff depositing mud and rock from an adjacent farm field, approximately 1 foot deep in some places, across Patit road. The flowing watr and mud also displaced a tractor in the field. Crops were also reported damaged from the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another trough system brought a round of slow moving stratiform slow moving showers. With the abundant amount of precipitation from earlier in the month, the streams and creeks were already near bank full. The additional 1 inch of precipitation starting to create problems for low lying areas in the Southeast WA and Lower ID Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low spots on Maple Street under I-90 flooded with standing water from heavy rains. Water was estimated to be under a foot deep. Several other areas around Spokane experienced standing water. Many of these were in known problem spots with poor drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure amplified over the Western US at the end of June, affecting the Pacific Northwest. This resulted in the first heatwave across Western Washington, with high temperatures climbing well into the 80s and even 90s. In addition to the heat, cold water was also a concern as people looked for relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record high temperature of 87 degrees was set at the Quillayute airport on Saturday June 25. This broke the previous record of 86 degrees set in 2017.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper trough pushed several shortwave troughs supported by a moderate plume of moisture in the middle of June.  Persistent rain showers that develop as a result of each shortwave trough produced enough rainfall for area rivers to swell, with minor flooding being observed along portions of Mill Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager relayed reports of flooding along Mill Creek between Thomas and Kooskooskie, WA. There were reports of water over roadways as well as pictures showing minor flooding on a property adjacent to Mill Creek. The creek and flood waters receded later on the June 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager for Walla Walla county relayed several reports of flooding within the area. Near Lanette Cir and Old Milton Hwy reports indicated that there was water over the roadway and that a property had flooding up to the house. Near Powerline road further east, reports of water over the roadways was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge brought warm temperatures region wide for the first half of September. This resulted in record high temperatures set on September 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quillayute ASOS recorded a record high temperature on September 10th of 92 degrees, this beat the previous recorded back in 2020 of 89 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started several wildfires in the Northwest Pasayten Wilderness, east of Ross Lake on September 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started several wildfires around the Parks Wildfire and formed the NW Pasayten Wilderness complex. It was east of Ross Lake. The complex burned 17,998 acres of rugged timber. Individual fires were Parks (12,163 acres), Kid (3,797 acres), Skagit (445 acres), Three Fools (1,112 acres), Shull Creek (426 acres).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swung through the region on the evening of the 22nd through the morning of the 23rd. It brought another round of widespread snow. It moved through quickly bringing most locations 1 to 3 inches. Localized heavy amounts of 6 and 7 inches were reported in the Cascades and Northwest Washington. Pockets of freezing rain impacted the Central Basin leading to wrecks and slide-offs with around a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff responded to fatal crash along Road B near Road 18 and 20. Ephrata had ice accumulations of above a tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Low moved through the Pacific Northwest at the end of November. With below normal temperatures of single digits and teens and ample moisture, it brought widespread snow. The heaviest snow fell along the Cascades and Northern Washington reaching South into Spokane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer recorded 10 inches of snow through 1200LST on the November 30. Other observations through the Okanogan Valley ranged from 6 to 12 inches.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave diving down northwest flow aloft combined with increasing moisture transport from the west resulted in heavy snow accumulations along the east slopes of the Washington Cascades overnight Dec. 19th through the evening of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bumping Ridge Snotel station reported 12 inches of new snow in a 13 hour period ending at 1200 PST on Dec. 20th. The Washington Department of Highways reported 15 inches of new snow in a 25 hour period ending at 1817 PST on Dec. 20th.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of surface frontal boundaries, warm air overrunning low level and valley cold pools, and a moderate transport of moisture in a west to southwest flow aloft resulted in impactful wintry conditions across southeast Washington, northeast and central Oregon. A mix of heavy freezing rain and heavy snow resulted in numerous traffic accidents and highway closures across the region during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 4 miles east southeast of Roslyn, WA measured 12 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 1139 PST on Dec. 24th. Via a public report 30 miles west of Tieton, 0.25 inches ice accumulation from freezing rain was reported at 1000 PST on Dec. 24th. A trained spotter 25 miles southwest of South Cle Elum, WA measured 0.25 inches of ice accumulation from freezing rain at 1402 PST on Dec. 24th. Interstate 90 was closed between Dec. 23rd and the 24th due to heavy ice accumulations up to Snoqualmie Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level trough impacting the Pacific Northwest placed the region under a moist southwest flow aloft, and combined with a strong cold air pooling along the eastern slopes of the Washington Cascades, resulted in heavy snow occurring in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 6 miles northwest of Thorp, Wa measured 9 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 1200 PST on Dec. 10th. Via social media, a member of the public in Ellensburg, Wa reported 4 inches of new snow at 2132 PST on Dec. 9th.
